{{Infobox ancient site |name = Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun |image = [[File:竜田御坊山3号墳 横口式石槨.JPG|280px]]
{{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}:附 designated)
{{small|(Left: lid and base stones, right: sealing stone. Exhibit at the {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)}} |alternate_name = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun |part_of = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan |latitude = 34.611361 |longitude = 135.726536 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} |size = Diameter 8m
Height 2.5m |burial_facility = {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}
(Containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|Tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}) |excavated_artifacts = Human remains, amber pillow, tri-colored lidded inkstone, glass writing tube |satellite_kofun = |construction_period = Mid-7th century |interred_persons = One young male
(Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Excavated artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Goboyama Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Topography Series|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30: Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} * {{Cite journal|author=Kinuhata Ayumu et al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial Context]}}|date=2021|journal=Kōkogaku Ronkō: Kashihara Archaeological Institute Bulletin|publisher={{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}}|volume=44|pages=1–18|ref={{Harvid|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum - Houses and exhibits the stone coffin and artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun. == External Links == * {{National Heritage Database|201|10002|Excavated Artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Ikaruga]] {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}:附 designated)
{{small|(Left: lid and base stones, right: sealing stone. Exhibit at the {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)}} |alternate_name = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun |part_of = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan |latitude = 34.611361 |longitude = 135.726536 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} |size = Diameter 8m
Height 2.5m |burial_facility = {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}
(Containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|Tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}) |excavated_artifacts = Human remains, amber pillow, tri-colored lidded inkstone, glass writing tube |satellite_kofun = |construction_period = Mid-7th century |interred_persons = One young male
(Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Excavated artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}:附 designated)
{{small|(Left: lid and base stones, right: sealing stone. Exhibit at the {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)}} |alternate_name = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun |part_of = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan |latitude = 34.611361 |longitude = 135.726536 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} |size = Diameter 8m

(Containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|Tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}) |excavated_artifacts = Human remains, amber pillow, tri-colored lidded inkstone, glass writing tube |satellite_kofun = |construction_period = Mid-7th century |interred_persons = One young male
(Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Excavated artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}:附 designated)
{{small|(Left: lid and base stones, right: sealing stone. Exhibit at the {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)}} |alternate_name = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun |part_of = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan |latitude = 34.611361 |longitude = 135.726536 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} |size = Diameter 8m
Height 2.5m |burial_facility = {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}
(Containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|Tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}) |excavated_artifacts = Human remains, amber pillow, tri-colored lidded inkstone, glass writing tube |satellite_kofun = |construction_period = Mid-7th century |interred_persons = One young male
(Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Excavated artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Goboyama Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Topography Series|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30: Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} * {{Cite journal|author=Kinuhata Ayumu et al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial Context]}}|date=2021|journal=Kōkogaku Ronkō: Kashihara Archaeological Institute Bulletin|publisher={{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}}|volume=44|pages=1–18|ref={{Harvid|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum - Houses and exhibits the stone coffin and artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun. == External Links == * {{National Heritage Database|201|10002|Excavated Artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Ikaruga]] It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} * {{Cite journal|author=Kinuhata Ayumu et al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial Context]}}|date=2021|journal=Kōkogaku Ronkō: Kashihara Archaeological Institute Bulletin|publisher={{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}}|volume=44|pages=1–18|ref={{Harvid|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum - Houses and exhibits the stone coffin and artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun. == External Links == * {{National Heritage Database|201|10002|Excavated Artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |name = Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun |image = [[File:竜田御坊山3号墳 横口式石槨.JPG|280px]]
{{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}:附 designated)
{{small|(Left: lid and base stones, right: sealing stone. Exhibit at the {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)}} |alternate_name = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun |part_of = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan |latitude = 34.611361 |longitude = 135.726536 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |size = Diameter 8m
Height 2.5m |burial_facility = {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}
(Containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|Tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}) |excavated_artifacts = Human remains, amber pillow, tri-colored lidded inkstone, glass writing tube |satellite_kofun = |construction_period = Mid-7th century |interred_persons = One young male
(Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Excavated artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Goboyama Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Topography Series|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30: Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} * {{Cite journal|author=Kinuhata Ayumu et al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial Context]}}|date=2021|journal=Kōkogaku Ronkō: Kashihara Archaeological Institute Bulletin|publisher={{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}}|volume=44|pages=1–18|ref={{Harvid|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum - {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |name = Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun |image = [[File:竜田御坊山3号墳 横口式石槨.JPG|280px]]
{{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}:附 designated)
{{small|(Left: lid and base stones, right: sealing stone. Exhibit at the {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)}} |alternate_name = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun |part_of = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan |latitude = 34.611361 |longitude = 135.726536 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |size = Diameter 8m
Height 2.5m |burial_facility = {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}
(Containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|Tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}) |excavated_artifacts = Human remains, amber pillow, tri-colored lidded inkstone, glass writing tube |satellite_kofun = |construction_period = Mid-7th century |interred_persons = One young male
(Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Excavated artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Goboyama Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Topography Series|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30: Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} * {{Cite journal|author=Kinuhata Ayumu et al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial Context]}}|date=2021|journal=Kōkogaku Ronkō: Kashihara Archaeological Institute Bulletin|publisher={{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}}|volume=44|pages=1–18|ref={{Harvid|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum - Houses and exhibits the stone coffin and artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun. == External Links == * {{National Heritage Database|201|10002|Excavated Artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Ikaruga]] revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |name = Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun |image = [[File:竜田御坊山3号墳 横口式石槨.JPG|280px]]
{{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}:附 designated)
{{small|(Left: lid and base stones, right: sealing stone. Exhibit at the {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)}} |alternate_name = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun |part_of = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan |latitude = 34.611361 |longitude = 135.726536 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |size = Diameter 8m
Height 2.5m |burial_facility = {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}
(Containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|Tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}) |excavated_artifacts = Human remains, amber pillow, tri-colored lidded inkstone, glass writing tube |satellite_kofun = |construction_period = Mid-7th century |interred_persons = One young male
(Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Excavated artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Goboyama Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Topography Series|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30: Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} * {{Cite journal|author=Kinuhata Ayumu et al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial Context]}}|date=2021|journal=Kōkogaku Ronkō: Kashihara Archaeological Institute Bulletin|publisher={{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}}|volume=44|pages=1–18|ref={{Harvid|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum - Houses and exhibits the stone coffin and artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun. == External Links == * {{National Heritage Database|201|10002|Excavated Artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Ikaruga]] (Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Excavated artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial Context]}}|date=2021|journal=Kōkogaku Ronkō: Kashihara Archaeological Institute Bulletin|publisher={{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'Burial Facility' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |name = Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun |image = [[File:竜田御坊山3号墳 横口式石槨.JPG|280px]]
{{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}} (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}:附 designated)
{{small|(Left: lid and base stones, right: sealing stone. Exhibit at the {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)}} |alternate_name = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun |part_of = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan |latitude = 34.611361 |longitude = 135.726536 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |size = Diameter 8m
Height 2.5m |Burial Facility = {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}
(Containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|Tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}) |excavated_artifacts = Human remains, amber pillow, tri-colored lidded inkstone, glass writing tube |satellite_kofun = |construction_period = Mid-7th century |interred_persons = One young male
(Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = excavations (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Goboyama Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Topography Series|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30: Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} * {{Cite journal|author=Kinuhata Ayumu et al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial Context]}}|date=2021|journal=Kōkogaku Ronkō: Kashihara Archaeological Institute Bulletin|publisher={{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}}|volume=44|pages=1–18|ref={{Harvid|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum - Houses and exhibits the stone coffin and artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun. == External Links == * {{National Heritage Database|201|10002|Excavated Artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Ikaruga]] (Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = excavations (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial (Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = excavations (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} Exhibit at the {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)}} |alternate_name = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun | part_of = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan |latitude = 34.611361 |longitude = 135.726536 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |size = Diameter 8m

(Containing a lacquered house-typed {{ill|Tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}) |excavated_artifacts = Human remains, amber pillow, tri-colored lidded inkstone, glass writing tube |satellite_kofun = |built = Mid-7th century |interred_persons = One young male
(Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = excavations (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-typed {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial Context]}}|date=2021|journal=Kōkogaku Ronkō: Kashihara Archaeological Institute Bulletin|publisher={{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}}|volume=44|pages=1–18|ref={{Harvid|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum - Houses and exhibits the stone coffin and artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun. == External Links == * {{National Heritage Database|201|10002|burial goods from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun}} Exhibit at the {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)}} |alternate_name = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun | part_of = Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan |latitude = 34.611361 |longitude = 135.726536 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |size = Diameter 8m
Height 2.5m |Burial Facility = {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}
(Containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|Tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}) |excavated_artifacts = Human remains, amber pillow, tri-colored lidded inkstone, glass writing tube |satellite_kofun = |built = Mid-7th century |interred_persons = One young male
(Hypothesis: a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family) |mausoleum = |historic_sites = None |tangible_cultural_properties = excavations (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = Mound no longer exists |map = Japan Nara |icon = Round tumulus |label = Tatsuta Goboyama
No. 3 Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun''' (竜田御坊山3号墳, also called Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun) was a [[Kofun]] located in {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|ja|斑鳩町}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, it was a {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}. The mound has since disappeared, but the excavated artifacts are designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview == Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun was a small round tumulus built on the southern slope of a hill in the northwestern Nara Basin, north of {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine (Nara)|ja|龍田神社}} and southwest of {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was part of the Tatsuta Goboyama Kofun Cluster, with two other tumuli (Nos. 1 and 2) located to the southwest. The tumulus was discovered during a residential development project in 1965 (Showa 40), after which it was excavated and subsequently destroyed{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The tumulus is estimated to have been circular, with a diameter of about 8 meters and a height of 2.5 meters{{Sfn|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}. The burial facility was a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, opening southward and containing a lacquered house-shaped {{ill|tōkan|ja|陶棺|1=Ceramic Coffins}}. The adoption of the Cave Tomb-style stone coffin and lacquered coffin indicates the high social status of the interred individual. However, the hastily prepared lacquered tōkan, with mismatched components and repair marks, suggests an emergency burial{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. Artifacts recovered from the coffin include human remains (a 14–15-year-old male), an amber pillow, a tri-colored lidded inkstone, and a glass writing tube, all of which are rare and noteworthy. The kofun is dated to the mid-7th century, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. The excavated artifacts were designated as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} in 1981 (Showa 56). == Burial Facility == The burial facility consisted of a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone coffin|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, constructed with granite. It comprised three main components: a base stone, a lid stone, and a sealing stone. The dimensions of these components are as follows{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}: * Lid stone: Maximum length 2.86 m, width 1.61 m, height 0.93 m * Base stone: Maximum length 2.93 m, width 1.67 m, height 0.57 m * Sealing stone: Maximum width 1.11 m, height 0.90 m, thickness 0.35 m The interior of the coffin was coated with plaster, enhancing its sealing capability. The lacquered tōkan inside the coffin, although of high status, showed signs of emergency preparation, with mismatched components and hasty repairs{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == Cultural Artifacts == Artifacts excavated from the tōkan include: * Amber pillow: Analyzed as originating from Kuji, Iwate Prefecture * Tri-colored lidded inkstone: Possibly imported from China or the Korean Peninsula * Glass writing tube: May correspond to writing tools described in Wang Xizhi’s "Pen Treatise" == Historical Context == The interred individual is hypothesized to be a member of the Upper Miyake Royal Family, descendants of Prince Shōtoku. The cluster’s proximity to key temples like {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}} suggests a connection to the region’s political and religious developments{{Sfn|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Goboyama Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Topography Series|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30: Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Goboyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} * {{Cite journal|author=Kinuhata Ayumu et al.|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kashikoken.jp/under_construction/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/kiyo44-kinuhata.pdf Reassessment of Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun and Its Burial Context]}}|date=2021|journal=Kōkogaku Ronkō: Kashihara Archaeological Institute Bulletin|publisher={{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}}|volume=44|pages=1–18|ref={{Harvid|Kinuhata et al.|2021}}}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum - Houses and exhibits the stone coffin and artifacts from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun. == External Links == * {{National Heritage Database|201|10002|burial goods from Tatsuta Goboyama No. 3 Kofun}}
